beginner.question: What is semantic HTML?
beginner.answer: Semantic HTML uses elements that describe their meaning, like <article>, <nav>, <section>, instead of generic <div> tags.

beginner.question: What is the <main> element used for?
beginner.answer: <main> represents the dominant content of the page. It should be unique and not nested within article, aside, footer, header, or nav.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <article> and <section>?
beginner.answer: <article> is self-contained content that can be syndicated (blog post, news story). <section> groups thematically related content within a page.

beginner.question: What does <nav> represent?
beginner.answer: <nav> represents a section of a page that links to other pages or parts within the page, typically a navigation menu.

beginner.question: What is the purpose of <aside>?
beginner.answer: <aside> represents content indirectly related to the main content, such as sidebars, callout boxes, or related links.

beginner.question: When should you use <header>?
beginner.answer: <header> represents introductory content. Use it for page headers, article headers, or section introductions.

beginner.question: When should you use <footer>?
beginner.answer: <footer> represents footer information like copyright, links, and author info. Use at page level or within article/section.

beginner.question: What is the <figure> element?
beginner.answer: <figure> represents self-contained content like images, diagrams, or code listings, optionally with a <figcaption>.

beginner.question: What is the <time> element?
beginner.answer: <time> represents a date or time. The datetime attribute provides a machine-readable format for better SEO and accessibility.

beginner.question: What does <address> represent?
beginner.answer: <address> provides contact information for its nearest <article> or <body> ancestor, like a physical address or email.

intermediate.question: How do semantic elements improve accessibility?
intermediate.answer: Semantic elements create ARIA landmarks automatically, allowing screen reader users to navigate by regions (banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo).

intermediate.question: What is the correct heading hierarchy?
intermediate.answer: Use one <h1> per page, followed by <h2>, <h3>, etc., without skipping levels. Headings should match the content structure.

intermediate.question: Can a page have multiple <header> elements?
intermediate.answer: Yes. A page can have one top-level <header> and additional <header> elements inside <article> or <section> elements.

intermediate.question: How does semantic HTML improve SEO?
intermediate.answer: Search engines use semantic structure to understand content hierarchy, identify main content, and extract metadata like dates and authors.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <b> and <strong>?
intermediate.answer: <strong> has semantic meaning (strong importance). <b> is presentational bold without semantic meaning.

intermediate.question: How should <section> be used?
intermediate.answer: <section> should group thematically related content and typically have a heading. Do not use it purely as a styling wrapper.

intermediate.question: What is the cite attribute on <blockquote>?
intermediate.answer: The cite attribute specifies the URL of the source document from which the quotation was taken.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <article> and <div>?
intermediate.answer: <article> has semantic meaning (self-contained content). <div> has no semantic meaning. Screen readers treat them differently.

intermediate.question: Can <main> be used multiple times on a page?
intermediate.answer: No. The <main> element should be unique per page. Use role='main' only once as well.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of <figcaption>?
intermediate.answer: <figcaption> provides a caption for the parent <figure> element. It is semantically linked to the figure content.

advanced.question: Explain the document outline algorithm for semantic HTML.
advanced.answer: The HTML5 outline algorithm creates a document outline from heading elements and sectioning content (article, section, nav, aside). Each sectioning element creates a new section in the outline.

advanced.question: How does the implicit ARIA role of <header> change when nested?
advanced.answer: When <header> is a top-level element (direct child of body), it has role='banner'. When nested inside <article> or <section>, it has no landmark role.

advanced.question: What happens when you nest <section> inside <article> vs <article> inside <section>?
advanced.answer: Both are valid. <section> inside <article> creates thematic sections within an article. <article> inside <section> groups independent articles under a theme.

advanced.question: How does the outline algorithm differ in practice between browsers?
advanced.answer: No browser fully implements the HTML5 outline algorithm. The actual accessibility tree uses ARIA roles and heading levels, not the document outline.

advanced.question: How do you test if your semantic HTML is correct for screen readers?
advanced.answer: Use a screen reader (NVDA, VoiceOver, JAWS) to navigate by landmarks. Use the Accessibility Tree in DevTools. Use axe DevTools or Lighthouse for automated testing.

advanced.question: What is the relationship between <article> and the RSS feed?
advanced.answer: <article> represents content suitable for syndication in feeds like RSS. This is its original semantic purpose — each article can be a feed item.

advanced.question: How do semantic elements interact with CSS grid and flexbox?
advanced.answer: Semantic elements are block-level by default and work identically to <div> in CSS layouts. They can be flex or grid items without issues.

advanced.question: What is the 'heading content' category in HTML?
advanced.answer: Heading content defines the heading of a section. Elements: <h1> through <h6> and <hgroup>. They are used to label sections in the document outline.

advanced.question: How does the <hgroup> element work and why was it redefined in HTML 5.3?
advanced.answer: <hgroup> groups multiple heading levels (e.g., a title and subtitle). In HTML 5.3, it was redefined to be a heading content element that contributes one heading to the outline.

advanced.question: What is the difference between <section> and <article> in terms of syndication?
advanced.answer: <article> is specifically designed for syndication (RSS, social media embeds). <section> is a thematic group but not intended for independent distribution.
